serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of boiled cauliflower is approximately 100 g (about one cup of florets), commonly served as a side vegetable or incorporated into a main dish.

nutrient_source.title: Cauliflower, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for boiled cauliflower (cr_id F14040004). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value for boiled cauliflower is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is estimated based on the closest analogous low-carbohydrate cruciferous vegetable entries (broccoli GI ~10–15; cauliflower raw GI ~15) documented in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 4.11) / 100 ≈ 0.6 per 100 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (15 mg/100 g) and meaningful potassium (142 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Boiled cauliflower is highly suitable for individuals with hypertension; its sodium content of 15 mg/100 g is well within DASH dietary guidelines, and its potassium-to-sodium ratio supports vascular blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (4.11 g/100 g), low sugar (2.08 g/100 g), and low estimated GI (~15)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Boiled cauliflower is an excellent choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes; its very low carbohydrate content, low glycaemic index (~15), and moderate dietary fibre (2.3 g/100 g) result in a negligible glycaemic load of approximately 0.6 per 100 g serving, supporting stable postprandial blood glucose levels.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.07 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and high vitamin C (44.3 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Boiled cauliflower is cardioprotective; its negligible saturated fat (0.07 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, low sodium, and high vitamin C content (44.3 mg/100 g) support endothelial health and are consistent with AHA dietary recommendations for cardiovascular disease prevention.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sulforaphane precursors and high vitamin C (44.3 mg/100 g) with anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Boiled cauliflower is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; as a cruciferous vegetable it provides glucosinolate-derived sulforaphane and vitamin C (44.3 mg/100 g), both of which have documented anti-inflammatory effects, while its negligible saturated fat and zero trans fat pose no pro-inflammatory risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boiled cauliflower is a minimally processed vegetable dish consisting of fresh cauliflower florets (Brassica oleracea var. botrytis) cooked by immersion in boiling water until tender, then drained without added salt or fat. The result is a soft, mild-flavoured vegetable with a slightly sweet, subtly sulphurous taste and a tender yet slightly firm texture. Per 100 g it provides only 23 kcal, 1.84 g protein, 0.45 g fat, and 4.11 g carbohydrate, making it one of the lowest-calorie cooked vegetables available. Dietary fibre is 2.3 g/100 g, supporting digestive health and satiety. Sodium is exceptionally low at 15 mg/100 g, making it ideal for DASH and low-sodium diets. Vitamin C content is notable at approximately 44 mg/100 g even after boiling, contributing to immune function and collagen synthesis. The estimated glycaemic index of approximately 15 places it firmly in the low-GI category, with a glycaemic load of only 0.6 per 100 g serving, making it an outstanding choice for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes. Boiled cauliflower is widely consumed across global cuisines as a side dish, in curries, soups, stir-fries, and as a low-carbohydrate rice or mash substitute. It is naturally vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. Its cruciferous phytochemical profile, including glucosinolates and sulforaphane precursors, provides additional anti-inflammatory and potentially chemoprotective benefits.
common_user_queries: Is boiled cauliflower good for diabetes?
How many calories in boiled cauliflower?
Can I eat cauliflower if I have high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of cauliflower?
Is cauliflower good for weight loss?
Can I eat cauliflower with rheumatoid arthritis?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and secondary nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (cr_id F14040004) at confidence 0.95. The food is an exact match. GI is estimated from the closest analogous cruciferous vegetable entries in the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008, which is the approved GI source; this introduces a minor estimation uncertainty but does not reduce overall confidence to medium given the very low carbohydrate content and well-established low-GI classification of cauliflower.
notes: Saturated fat (0.07 g), monounsaturated fat (0.03 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.22 g), trans fat (0.0 g), potassium (142 mg), vitamin C (44.3 mg), vitamin A (0.0 mcg RAE), vitamin B1 (0.05 mg), vitamin B2 (0.06 mg), vitamin B3 (0.51 mg), vitamin B6 (0.18 mg), vitamin B12 (0.0 mcg), folate (44 mcg DFE), vitamin D (0.0 mcg), vitamin E (0.08 mg), phosphorus (40 mg), magnesium (15 mg), zinc (0.27 mg), selenium (0.6 mcg), and water (92.1 g) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cauliflower cooked boiled drained without salt (FDC ID 169986) as a supplementary reference to fill fields not reported in the HPB entry; these values are consistent with the HPB primary source. Vitamin A, vitamin B12, vitamin D, and cholesterol are reported as 0.0 for boiled cauliflower, which is confirmed by both HPB and USDA FoodData Central — cauliflower naturally contains negligible retinol/RAE, no B12 (plant food), no vitamin D, and no cholesterol (plant food). Trans fat is 0.0 confirmed — plant-based food with no added fats. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct boiled cauliflower GI entry exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving = (15 × 4.11) / 100 = 0.62, rounded to 0.6. The input record listed 'keto' as a dietary tag; this is supported by the very low net carbohydrate content (approximately 1.8 g net carbs per 100 g after subtracting fibre). No -1 sentinel values remain unexplained; all fields set to -1 in the template have been populated with values from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as a supplementary approved source.
